            # DAG Walker Directive

## Slug
picard-alb

## Seed
thm:albanese_universal_property

## Strategy context
The positive-genus arm of the Jacobian witness (`def:positiveGenusWitness`)
consumes Route A: the Picard scheme via FGA representability, plus the Albanese
universal property of Pic^0. The two route heads are
`thm:fga_pic_representability` (Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex) and
`thm:albanese_universal_property` (Albanese_AlbaneseUP.tex). Beneath them sits
the entire Picard/Albanese build-out: the relative Picard functor and its étale
sheafification, the Quot scheme and flattening stratification, relative Spec,
the tensor-object substrate (sheaf of k-modules, dual/inverse, internal hom),
line-bundle pullback/coherence, Pic^0 as an abelian variety (tangent space,
smoothness, properness, geometric irreducibility), the identity component, and
the Albanese descent chain (symmetric powers → Abel–Jacobi → Auslander–Buchsbaum
→ codim-1 extension → Thm 3.2 rational-map extension → coheight bridge). These
chapters have substantial intra-chapter wiring already (TensorObjSubstrate is the
best-wired chapter in the project) but the heads and many cross-chapter edges are
missing, leaving the Route-A cone disconnected from the spine.

## Depth / scope
**Your write domain is ONLY chapters matching `Picard_*.tex` and
`Albanese_*.tex`.** Three parallel walkers own the other regions — do NOT edit
Jacobian/AbelJacobi/Genus, Rigidity, or Cohomology/RiemannRoch chapters.

Walk UP from BOTH route heads — `thm:albanese_universal_property` AND
`thm:fga_pic_representability` — and make the Picard+Albanese cone complete:

1. For each isolated node in your chapters (use `archon dag-query ancestors` on
   both heads and `leandag show isolated --json` filtered to your chapters), add
   the `\uses{}` edges its proof actually invokes, intra- and inter-chapter
   within your region.
2. Wire the FGA representability chain: `thm:fga_pic_representability` `\uses`
   the relative Picard functor + étale sheafification (Picard_RelPicFunctor),
   the Quot/Hilbert prerequisites (Picard_QuotScheme,
   Picard_FlatteningStratification), relative Spec (Picard_RelativeSpec), and the
   line-bundle quot correspondence. Then `thm:pic0_smooth` / `thm:pic0_proper` /
   the Pic^0-as-abelian-variety chain (Picard_Pic0AbelianVariety,
   Picard_IdentityComponent) `\uses` representability + the tensor-object
   substrate (Picard_TensorObjSubstrate) and line-bundle chapters
   (Picard_LineBundlePullback, Picard_LineBundleCoherence,
   Picard_SheafOverEquivalence).
3. Wire the Albanese descent chain: `thm:albanese_universal_property` `\uses` the
   symmetric-power / Abel–Jacobi morphism lemmas (Albanese_AlbaneseUP) and the
   indeterminacy-extension chain (Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension,
   Albanese_CodimOneExtension, Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum,
   Albanese_CoheightBridge).
4. Where the cone bottoms out at a Mathlib result with no project block, author a
   Mathlib dependency anchor (`\mathlibok` + `\lean{<real decl>}` +
   `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`) ONLY if you can name the genuine Mathlib
   declaration; otherwise report it.

**Boundary rule:** `def:positiveGenusWitness` (Jacobian.tex) is owned by the
Backbone walker, which adds the edges down to your two route heads. You do NOT
edit Jacobian.tex. Stop at the top of your region; record the handoff.

For any node in your chapters with a real statement but no `\lean{}` and not a
`\begin{remark}`, add a placeholder `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.<name>}`. The
`lem:quot_boundedness` / `lem:generic_flatness_algebraic` family and similar
decomposition aids should get placeholder pins rather than remaining unpinned.

Do NOT add `\leanok`. Do NOT invent new mathematics; transcribe dependencies.
Genuinely proof-less ∞ nodes (Route-A representability frontier) go under "Could
not complete" — those are known prover-frontier items, not your job to prove.

## References
- `references/kleiman-picard.md` (Kleiman, FGA, §4 existence, §5 Pic^0, §6 Pic^τ).
- `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md` (Nitsure, Quot/Hilbert construction).
- `references/abelian-varieties.md` (Milne, Albanese UP Prop 6.1/6.4 §III.6).
- `references/stacks-constructions.md` (relative-spec tags — see caveats in the
  pointer file). Cite verbatim from the local files only; never from memory.

1	---
2	name: dag-walker
3	description: Walk UP a target declaration's dependency cone in the leandag graph and make it complete — every dependency has a statement, an informal proof (finite effort, not ∞), and a complete \uses{} set. Turns ∞-effort cones into finite ones and makes the frontier trustworthy. Plan/dag-agent-dispatched against a seed node.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against a SEED declaration whose foundation you want made
10	    complete: an ∞-source (`archon dag-query gaps`), the project goal, or a
11	    frontier node you suspect has an incomplete `\uses{}` list. Name the seed
12	    label in the directive (`## Seed`). I walk UP from there.
13	  - I am the "is the foundation under this target complete?" agent —
14	    complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise
15	    direction). I follow the dependency graph wherever it leads, across
16	    chapters, filling whatever the cone is missing. Give me a wide write
17	    domain when the cone spans chapters:
18	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex'
19	    and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a
20	    source (so I can spawn a reference-retriever).
21	  - Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't
22	    trust: a node looks ready when its `\uses{}` under-declares its real
23	    dependencies. I close that gap so the injected frontier is honest.
24	  - My report's "Could not complete" section lists dependencies that are
25	    genuinely hard/novel (no informal proof exists yet). Those are strategy
26	    items — read STRATEGY.md implications before sending a prover at them.
27	  - **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** — that is the deterministic
28	    sync_leanok phase's job. `\mathlibok` I may add, but only on genuine
29	    Mathlib dependency anchors (see my body).
30	---
31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
87	
88	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
89	
90	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
91	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
94	
95	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
96	
97	## What you must / must not do
98	
99	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
100	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
101	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
102	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
103	
104	## Workflow
105	
106	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
107	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
108	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
109	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
110	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
111	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
112	7. Write your report.
113	
114	## Logging
115	
116	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
117	
118	```markdown
119	# DAG Walker Report
120	
121	## Slug
122	<slug>
123	
124	## Seed
125	<label>
126	
127	## Status
128	<COMPLETE — cone fully grounded | PARTIAL — genuine gaps remain (listed below)>
129	
130	## Cone before → after
131	- ∞ holes: <N before> → <M after>
132	- broken \uses: <N before> → <M after>
133	- blocks added: <count>;  \uses edges added: <count>
134	
135	## Blocks added / proofs written
136	- `\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>
137	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
138	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
139	
140	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
141	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
142	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
143	
144	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
145	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
146	
147	## References consulted
148	- `references/<file>.md` — <what verbatim content you took>
149	
150	## Notes for dispatcher
151	- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>
152	```
153	
154	## Return Value
155	
156	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.
157

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);

59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}

394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}

481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\section{Lean encoding}
512	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
513	
514	The Lean target file is the new
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
518	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
519	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
520	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
521	
522	\begin{itemize}
523	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
524	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
525	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
526	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
527	    \texttt{representable} below.
528	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
529	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
530	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
531	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
532	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
533	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
534	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
535	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
536	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
537	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
538	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
539	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
540	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
541	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
542	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
543	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
544	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
545	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
546	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
547	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
548	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
549	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
550	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
551	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
552	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
553	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
554	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
555	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
556	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
557	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
558	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
559	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
560	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
561	\end{itemize}
562	
563	No new core axiom is required: this chapter is an assembly chapter on
564	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
565	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single
566	large external input (the Altman--Kleiman theorem of effective
567	flat-and-proper equivalence relations on quasi-projective schemes) is
568	recorded as \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} and is itself a standard
569	result; if its Lean formalisation is not yet in Mathlib, the
570	\texttt{smoothProperQuotient} Lean declaration may be sorried and the
571	gap reported to the plan agent, as it is logically downstream of the
572	Hilbert-scheme construction of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.

574	\section{Sorry-by-sorry closure order}
575	\label{sec:fga_pic_sorry_closure_order}
576	
577	% iter-200 plan agent: stale-prose findings from iter-199
578	% lean-vs-blueprint-checker fga-iter199 addressed below (intro paragraph,
579	% Sorry 4 subsection location, closure-order summary Sorry 4 motivation).
580	The Lean target file
581	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} currently
582	carries seven open sorries, all of them \texttt{⟨sorry⟩} instance
583	bodies for the seven carrier typeclasses
584	\texttt{HasPicSharp}, \texttt{HasDivFunctor}, \texttt{HasPicScheme},
585	\texttt{HasAbelMap}, \texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient},
586	\texttt{PicSharpRepresentable}, \texttt{PicSchemeGroupObject}.
587	After the iter-199 carrier-soundness refactor the file is
588	structurally homogeneous: every sorry is isolated in a single
589	\texttt{⟨sorry⟩} instance constructor of a \texttt{Prop}-valued
590	typeclass, so every theorem body extracts cleanly from the
591	appropriate field of the corresponding typeclass and is itself
592	axiom-clean. The carrier-soundness refactor of the
593	preceding section isolates each typeclass instance as the unique
594	sorry-carrying site for its associated extracted definition, so a
595	consumer that abstracts over the typeclass is kernel-clean. This
596	section is the explicit closure-order plan: for each sorry we record
597	(i) the Lean location and identifier, (ii) the mathematical content
598	to be proved, (iii) the Mathlib prerequisites at the project's pinned
599	revision, (iv) the Route~C (Riemann--Roch) substrate dependency, and
600	(v) a closure-order rank, where rank~1 is an independent carrier that
601	can be discharged from existing Mathlib alone, rank~2 depends on a
602	prior carrier or a Mathlib gap that is not Riemann--Roch, and rank~3
603	depends on a Route~C substrate re-engagement.
604	
605	\medskip
606	
607	The ranks are not a serialisation: rank-1 carriers may be closed in
608	parallel; rank-2 and rank-3 carriers depend either on other carriers
609	in the same chapter, on sibling A.1.c / A.2.b chapters
610	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
611	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), or on the Route~C arc of the project
612	strategy (the Riemann--Roch development for smooth proper curves over
613	a field). The strategic point of the ranks is to identify which
614	sorries can be attempted first while the Route~C arc remains
615	substrate-blocked.

799	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
800	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
801	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
802	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
803	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
804	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.

806	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
807	\begin{itemize}
808	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
809	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:

812	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
813	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
814	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
815	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
816	    isolated as a named result.
817	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
818	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
819	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
820	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
821	    not in Mathlib in any form.
822	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
823	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
824	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
825	\end{itemize}

831	substantial development in its own right (corresponds to roughly
832	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the
833	flat-and-proper descent of equivalence relations from EGA IV~17).

835	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Two routes are available:

465	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0
466	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the
467	  identity).}
468	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
469	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
470	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
471	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme
472	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.
473	\end{theorem}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
477	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
478	        thm:fga_pic_representability}
479	  Apply the abstract identity-component substrate to the \(k\)-group
480	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
481	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By
482	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}, the
483	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite
484	  type and geometrically irreducible over \(k\). The base-change
485	  compatibility of
486	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} identifies
487	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\),
488	  closing the proof.
489	\end{proof}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
% identity-component substrate.)
% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then

content: The mechanism is now clear: **only statement-block `\uses{}` create edges; proof-block `\uses{}` are ignored.** This explains the isolated heads. Let me record this crucial finding and check what the FGA/Albanese chapters cite.

input.content:
---
name: leandag-uses-statement-block-only
description: leandag builds graph edges ONLY from \uses{} inside the statement environment; \uses{} in \begin{proof} blocks are ignored.
metadata:
  type: reference
---

leandag's parser (`leandag/parser.py` `_extract_declarations` + `dag.py` `_build_nodes` line ~189 `uses = decl.uses`) extracts each node's dependency edges from the **first `\uses{}` found inside the declaration environment body** (`\begin{theorem}...\end{theorem}`, `\begin{lemma}`, `\begin{definition}`, etc.).

**`\uses{}` placed inside a `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` block is parsed into `proof_tex` but NEVER contributes graph edges.** This is the opposite of the usual leanblueprint convention.

Consequences for DAG-walker work in this repo:
- A node is "isolated" / has no deps in `archon dag-query` whenever its dependency `\uses{}` live only in its proof block (very common here — the two route heads `thm:fga_pic_representability` and `thm:albanese_universal_property` were isolated for exactly this reason while their proofs `\cref`/`\uses` many siblings).
- To wire an edge A→B (A uses B), put `\uses{B}` in A's **statement** environment, not its proof.
- Multiline `\uses{...,\n ...}` works fine (regex `[^}]+` spans newlines). Only the FIRST `\uses{}` in the environment is read, so list all deps in one block.
- After editing, run `leandag build` then verify with the `.leandag/dag.json` edges (`{from: dep, to: dependent}`) — `archon dag-query` may read the same dag.json. Edge direction: `from` = the used/dependency node, `to` = the using/dependent node.

2	\label{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}

4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174 / refined iter-175). This chapter is the A.4.d
6	% sub-row of Route~A (the positive-genus arm of
7	% \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the join of two preceding rows:
8	% A.3 (the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of the Picard scheme together
9	% with its degree map; the relevant Picard-scheme representability content
