146	    lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
147	    lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
148	    lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
149	    lem:pullback_unit_iso, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}
150	
151	
152	\leanok
153	  The construction proceeds in four steps over the locally-trivial carrier
154	  \[
155	    \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) \;=\;
156	      \bigl\{\, M : \text{a module on } C \times_k T \;\bigm|\;
157	      M \text{ is locally trivial} \,\bigr\}
158	  \]
159	  of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, on whose isomorphism classes the
160	  relative Picard quotient is taken. It is authored \emph{in parallel} against
161	  two inputs being built concurrently: the locally-trivial pullback--tensor
162	  comparison isomorphism \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} (consumed in
163	  Step~2 to make \(\pi_T^*\) additive) and the tensor inverse
164	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (consumed in Step~1 for group
165	  closure); both are themselves deferred targets.
166	
167	  \emph{Step 1 (abelian group on the carrier).} On isomorphism classes of the
168	  carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\), addition is the descent of the
169	  scheme-level tensor product of line bundles,
170	  \[
171	    [L] + [L'] \;:=\; [\, L \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} L' \,],
172	  \]
173	  where \(L \otimes L'\) is the locally-trivial descent of the scheme-level
174	  tensor product (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}); the sum lands again in
175	  the carrier because the tensor product of two locally-trivial modules is
176	  locally trivial (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The zero
177	  element is the class \([\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\) of the structure sheaf,
178	  the monoidal unit, which is locally trivial. Associativity, commutativity,
179	  and left/right unitality of \(+\) descend from the scheme-level coherence
180	  isomorphisms --- the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the
181	  braiding (\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}), and the unitors
182	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) --- exactly as for the absolute Picard group
183	  on tensor-invertible isomorphism classes
184	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The additive inverse is
185	  \[
186	    -[L] \;:=\; [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \,],
187	  \]
188	  where \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the witness supplied by
189	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}: it returns a \emph{locally-trivial}
190	  module together with an isomorphism
191	  \(L \otimes L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\), so the inverse
192	  stays inside the loc-triv carrier and the group is closed under negation.
193	
194	  \emph{Step 2 (the pullback homomorphism \(\pi_T^*\)).} Pullback along the
195	  projection \(\pi_T\) gives an additive map
196	  \[
197	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T),
198	  \]
199	  whose underlying assignment of line bundles is
200	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}. It is a homomorphism of abelian groups:
201	  it sends the unit to the unit, since
202	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{O}_T \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) by the unconditional
203	  pullback--unit isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}); and it is additive,
204	  since pullback commutes with tensor product on locally-trivial modules,
205	  \[
206	    \pi_T^*(N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} N') \;\cong\;
207	      \pi_T^* N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^* N',
208	  \]
209	  by the locally-trivial comparison isomorphism
210	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} specialised to the projection
211	  \(\pi_T\) on the \(\mathrm{OnProduct}\) carrier. This is the exact analogue,
212	  for the geometric pullback, of the additive monoid homomorphism on Picard
213	  groups induced by a ring map respecting tensor product, and is modelled on it
214	  field-for-field. Write \(H_T := \mathrm{im}(\pi_T^*)\) for its image, a
215	  subgroup of the carrier's isomorphism-class group.
216	
217	  \emph{Step 3 (setoid reconciliation).} The set-valued quotient of
218	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is taken modulo the
219	  isomorphism-class equivalence that declares two line bundles related when
220	  their classes differ by an element of \(H_T\). This relation coincides with
221	  the left-coset relation of the subgroup \(H_T\) inside the abelian group of
222	  Steps~1--2: both encode
223	  \[
224	    L \sim L' \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
225	      [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \otimes L' \,] \in H_T .
226	  \]
227	  Establishing this equivalence of relations is a concrete isomorphism-class
228	  argument over the carrier; it consumes the tensor inverse (Step~1) and the
229	  group law, but not the computational content of the comparison isomorphism.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 4 (transport).} By Step~3 the quotient set underlying
232	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is in canonical bijection with the group quotient
233	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) / H_T\), which carries the canonical
234	  abelian-group structure of a quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup.
235	  Transporting this structure along the bijection of Step~3 endows
236	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) with its abelian-group structure, under which the
237	  canonical surjection
238	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a
239	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
240	\end{proof}

427	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
428	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
429	
430	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
431	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
432	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
433	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
434	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
435	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
436	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
437	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
438	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

557	  presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to
558	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is nonempty. The full canonical statement
559	  --- that this morphism is the canonical sheafification unit and
560	  carries the universal property characterising
561	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) as the initial \'etale sheaf of abelian
562	  groups receiving a presheaf morphism from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\), so
563	  in particular that the abelian-group structure on
564	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is uniquely determined --- is recorded
565	  separately as \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical} below.
566	\end{theorem}
567	
568	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
569	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
570	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
571	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
572	% \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves filtered colimits, and the
573	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
574	% Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) machinery.
575	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
576	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure at
577	% b80f227 supplies a zero natural transformation as the Nonempty
578	% witness
579	% (\(\langle 0\rangle : \texttt{Nonempty}\,(\texttt{presheaf}\ C
580	%   \Rightarrow \texttt{etSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\)),
581	% NOT the canonical sheafification unit. The Lean statement type has
582	% been weakened to bare \texttt{Nonempty} existence to admit this
583	% trivial witness; the canonical morphism with its universal property
584	% is the content of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical} and
585	% is deferred. The closure is gated on the upstream Mathlib
586	% \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO NOT promote this
587	% block to \leanok-on-the-canonical-statement via the deterministic
588	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in review
589	% beyond the weak \texttt{Nonempty} content, until the body is
590	% replaced with the canonical sheafification unit and the prose is
591	% upgraded accordingly.
592	\begin{proof}
593	
594	
595	
596	
597	\leanok
598	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
599	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
600	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
601	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the
602	  hom-set of presheaf morphisms between these two objects is therefore
603	  nonempty. The full universal property of this canonical morphism
604	  --- and consequently the claim that the abelian-group structure on
605	  the sheafification is uniquely pinned by it --- is the content of
606	  the forward-looking \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
607	  below; see its proof sketch for the canonical formulation.
608	\end{proof}
609	
610	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
611	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
612	
613	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
614	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
615	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
616	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
617	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
618	  % property)
619	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
620	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
621	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
622	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
623	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
624	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
625	  % topologies  by
626	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
627	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
628	  %  \end{dfn}
629	  %  [\dots]
630	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
631	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
632	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
633	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
634	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
635	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
636	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
637	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
638	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
639	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
640	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
641	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
642	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
643	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
644	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
645	  \[
646	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
647	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
648	  \]
649	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
650	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
651	  of presheaves of abelian groups
652	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
653	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
654	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
655	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
656	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
657	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
658	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
659	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
660	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
661	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
662	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
663	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
664	  \(\eta\).
665	\end{theorem}
666	
667	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
668	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
669	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
670	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
671	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
672	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
673	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
674	\begin{proof}
675	
676	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
677	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
678	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
679	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
680	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
681	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
682	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
683	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
684	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
685	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
686	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
687	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
688	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
689	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
690	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
691	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
692	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
693	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
694	\end{proof}
695	
696	\section{Lean encoding}
697	\label{sec:relpic_lean_encoding}
698	
699	The Lean target file is the new
700	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}, sibling to
701	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
702	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}) and
703	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
704	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
705	Lean targets as follows.
706	\begin{itemize}
707	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
708	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}) is the abelian-group instance on the
709	    quotient set built in
710	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
711	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). Mathlib's
712	    \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} machinery on a normal subgroup of an abelian
713	    group is the backbone; the project-side work is to certify that
714	    \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism (one extra
715	    \texttt{LinearMap.map\_mul}--style line on tensor product preservation).
716	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
717	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}) is the group-valued presheaf
718	    \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\). The Lean form is a functor
719	    \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\) built from the data of
720	    \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} +
721	    \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
722	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
723	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}) packages the structure-map
724	    homomorphism property: the set map \(g^\sharp\) of
725	    \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a group homomorphism. On the Lean side
726	    this is a one-step strengthening of the existing set-valued naturality
727	    proof.
728	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
729	    (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) wraps the previous three lemmas as a
730	    functor instance into \(\AddCommGroup\).
731	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
732	    % NOTE (iter-176 review): renamed from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme``
733	    % to avoid a collision with the representing-scheme declaration of the same
734	    % name in ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability``.
735	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}) is the \'etale sheafification
736	    of \texttt{PicSharp}. The Lean form is
737	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf} applied to the big \'etale
738	    topology on \texttt{Scheme} with target category
739	    \texttt{AddCommGrpCat}.
740	    \textbf{Lean API note.} The big \'etale Grothendieck topology on the
741	    category of schemes is
742	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.etaleTopology}, of type
743	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme}
744	    (module \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Sites.Etale}; an
745	    \texttt{abbrev} for the topology generated by the big \'etale
746	    pretopology \texttt{Scheme.etalePretopology}). The sheafification
747	    entry point with abelian-group target is
748	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf
749	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}, of type
750	    \texttt{Functor (Schemeᵒᵖ \(\Rightarrow\) AddCommGrpCat)
751	    (Sheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat)} (module
752	    \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheafification}); it is
753	    available under a \texttt{CategoryTheory.HasWeakSheafify} instance
754	    for the pair \texttt{(etaleTopology, AddCommGrpCat)}. The slice
755	    \((\Sch/k)\) on the Lean side is captured by working over the

783	    AddCommGrpCat)}. The proof is structural: it identifies

798	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by

804	
805	\section{Out of scope}

1	\chapter{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-184). This chapter is the A.3 sub-row of Route~A

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94	
95	% The four conclusions of Kleiman \S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) are split below
96	% into per-conclusion theorem blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin to
97	% a Lean declaration. The verbatim source quote of lem:agps~(3) is
98	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
99	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference
100	% that quote rather than duplicating it. Each theorem carries its own
101	% \begin{proof} block (the matching paragraph of Kleiman's proof of
102	% lem:agps~(3)); the verbatim \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment from
103	% Kleiman's proof, covering all four conclusions, precedes the proof of
104	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.

106	\begin{theorem}\leanok
107	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
108	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
109	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
110	
111	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
112	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
113	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
114	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
115	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
116	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
117	  % extending \(k\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
119	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
120	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
121	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
122	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
123	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
124	\end{theorem}
125	
126	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
127	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
128	% (3).)
129	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
130	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
131	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
132	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
133	% \(G^0\) is open too.
134	%
135	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
136	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
137	% extending \(k\).
138	%
139	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
140	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
141	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
142	%
143	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
144	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
145	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
146	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
147	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
148	% [\dots]
149	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
150	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
151	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
152	% [\dots]
153	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
154	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
155	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
156	\begin{proof}
157	  \uses{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
158	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (clopen-subscheme part).
159	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
160	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
161	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
162	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
163	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
164	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
165	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
166	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
167	\end{proof}

169	\begin{theorem}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
173	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
175	
176	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
177	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
178	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
179	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
180	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
181	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
182	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
183	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
184	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
185	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
186	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
187	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
188	  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
189	\end{theorem}
190	
191	\begin{proof}
192	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
193	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (subgroup part); the
194	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
195	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
196	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
197	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
198	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
199	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
200	  image lies in the clopen identity component \(G^0\)
201	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence \(G^0\) is closed
202	  under the group operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)):
203	  the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of
204	  \(k\)-group schemes, and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme
205	  structure from \(G\).
206	\end{proof}

208	\begin{theorem}
209	
210	\leanok
211	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
212	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
213	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
214	
215	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
216	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
217	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
218	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
219	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
220	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
221	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
222	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
223	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
224	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
225	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
226	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
227	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
228	\end{theorem}
229	
230	\begin{proof}
231	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
232	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (finite-type and
233	  geometric-irreducibility part); the verbatim source quote is
234	  reproduced at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
235	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
236	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
237	  commutes with field extension,
238	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Over
239	  \(\overline k\), the product
240	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
241	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
242	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
243	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
244	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
245	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
246	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
247	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
248	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
249	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
250	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
251	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
252	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
253	\end{proof}
254	
255	\begin{theorem}
256	
257	\leanok
258	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
259	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
260	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
261	
262	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
263	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
264	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
265	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
266	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
267	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
268	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
269	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
270	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
271	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
272	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
273	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
274	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
275	\end{theorem}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
279	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (base-change part); the
280	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
281	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
282	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
283	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\): the identity component is
284	  connected and carries a \(k\)-rational section, so it is geometrically
285	  connected by
286	  \cref{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section} (the
287	  Stacks 037Q criterion; cf.\ EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). In particular for
288	  any field extension \(K/k\) the base change \((G^0)_K\) is connected,
289	  contains the image of the identity section of \(G_K\), and is open and
290	  closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and closed-immersion both descend under
291	  base change, \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence
292	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
293	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
294	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
295	\end{proof}

315	\begin{lemma}
316	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
317	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
319	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
320	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
321	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
322	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
323	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
324	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
325	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
326	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
327	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
328	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
329	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
330	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
331	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
332	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
333	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
334	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
335	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
336	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
337	  connected.
338	\end{lemma}
339	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
340	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
341	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
342	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
343	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
344	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
345	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
346	% ring is a field is connected.''
347	\begin{proof}
348	  % DAG iter-266: removed spurious \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
349	  % — the Stacks 037Q/04KV argument uses only connectedness of |X| and the
350	  % k-rational section, not the identity-component clopen theorem.
351	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
352	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
353	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
354	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
355	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
356	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
357	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
358	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
359	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
360	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
361	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
362	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
363	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
364	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
365	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
366	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
367	  is therefore connected.
368	
369	  Spelled out: this lemma is the iter-194 first-class formalisation of
370	  the Stacks 037Q iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1) specialised to the
371	  presence of a \(k\)-rational section (which automatically supplies
372	  the \enquote{algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(K\) equals \(k\)}
373	  condition via the section's pullback on global sections — see
374	  Stacks 037R for that step).
375	\end{proof}

386	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
387	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
388	
389	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
390	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
391	
392	\begin{definition}\leanok
393	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
394	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
396	  
397	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
398	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
399	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
400	  % L2921-L2929).
401	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
402	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
403	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
404	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
405	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
406	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
407	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
408	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
409	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
410	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
411	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
412	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
413	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
414	  % \(k\).
415	  %  \end{prp}"
416	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
417	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
418	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
419	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
420	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
421	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
422	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
423	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
424	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
425	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
426	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
427	  extension of the base field.
428	\end{definition}
429	
430	\section{The degree map}
431	\label{sec:pic_degree}
432	
433	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
434	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
435	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
436	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
437	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
438	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
439	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
440	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
441	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
442	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
443	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
444	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
445	degree.
446	
447	\begin{definition}\leanok
448	[Degree of a Picard class]
449	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
450	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
451	  
452	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
453	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
454	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
455	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
456	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
457	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
458	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
459	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
460	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
461	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
462	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
463	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
464	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
465	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
466	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
467	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
468	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
469	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
470	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
471	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
472	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
473	  \[
474	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
475	  \]
476	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
477	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
478	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
479	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
480	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
481	  \[
482	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
483	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
484	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
485	  \]
486	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
487	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
488	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
489	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
490	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
491	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
492	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
493	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
494	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
495	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
496	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
497	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
498	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
499	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
500	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
501	  of degree \(d\).
502	\end{definition}
503	
504	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
505	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
506	
507	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
508	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
509	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
510	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
511	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
512	automatically.
513	
514	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
515	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
516	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
517	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
518	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
519	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
520	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
521	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
522	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
523	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. Each theorem carries its
524	% own \begin{proof} block: the Kleiman th:qpp\&p source-proof quote
525	% precedes the proof of \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}, and the
526	% Cor.~cor:sm source-proof quote precedes the proof of
527	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}.
528	
529	\begin{theorem}\leanok
530	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
531	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
533	
534	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
535	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
536	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
537	  % L3990-L3996).
538	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
539	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
540	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
541	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
542	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
543	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
544	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
545	  % \end{ex}"
546	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
547	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
548	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
549	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
550	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
551	  % Exercise~REF."
552	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
553	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
554	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
555	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
556	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
557	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
558	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
559	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
560	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
561	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
562	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
563	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
564	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
565	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
566	  separately stated.)
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
570	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
571	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
572	% identity-component substrate.)
573	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
574	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
575	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
576	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
577	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
578	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
579	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
580	\begin{proof}
581	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism, thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible, thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes, thm:fga_pic_representability, thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
582	  We assemble the four conjuncts of the abelian-variety structure on
583	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) from four inputs.
584	
585	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
586	  By
587	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
588	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
589	  type by
590	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
591	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
592	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
593	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
594	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
595	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
596	
597	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
598	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
599	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
600	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
601	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
602	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
603	
604	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
605	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
606	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
607	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
608	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
609	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
610	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
611	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
612	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
613	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
614	
615	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
616	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
617	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
618	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
619	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
620	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
621	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
622	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
623	
624	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
625	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
626	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
627	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
628	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
629	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
630	\end{proof}
631	
632	\begin{theorem}
633	
634	\leanok
635	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
636	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
637	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
638	
639	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
640	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
641	  % PDF p.~92).
642	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
643	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
644	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
645	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
646	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
647	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
648	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
649	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
650	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
651	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
652	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
653	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
654	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
655	  \[
656	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
657	  \]
658	\end{theorem}
659	
660	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
661	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
662	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
663	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
664	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
665	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
666	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
667	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
668	% everywhere."
669	\begin{proof}
670	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, def:genus}
671	  By Kleiman~\S 5,
672	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
673	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
674	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
675	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
676	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. The abelian-variety structure of
677	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety} (step (iv) of its proof)
678	  shows \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds
679	  on the open identity component, and
680	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
681	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
682	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
683	\end{proof}
684	
685	\begin{theorem}
686	
687	\leanok
688	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
689	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
690	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
691	
692	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
693	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
694	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
695	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
696	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
697	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
698	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
699	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
700	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
701	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
702	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
703	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
704	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
705	  if and only if the degree
706	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
707	  vanishes. Equivalently,
708	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
709	\end{theorem}
710	
711	\begin{proof}
712	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:pic_zero_subscheme, def:divisor_degree_pic}
713	  A class
714	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
715	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
716	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
717	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, specialised to
718	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) --- precisely when
719	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
720	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
721	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
722	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the
723	  abelian variety of \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}, this image
724	  is exactly the degree-zero locus on \(k\)-points. Hence
725	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
726	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
727	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
728	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
729	  zero on \(C\)'').
730	\end{proof}

743	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
744	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme

746	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,

798	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
799	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
800	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
801	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
802	    underlying scheme).
803	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
804	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
805	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
806	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
807	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
808	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
809	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
810	\end{itemize}
811	
812	The abstract identity-component substrate
813	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
814	conclusion-specific theorems
815	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
816	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
817	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
818	\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
819	is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
820	follow-up iteration. The downstream
821	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
822	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
823	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
824	Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
825	live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
826	smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.
827	
828	\section{Out of scope}
829	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
830	
831	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
832	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
833	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
834	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
835	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
836	
837	\begin{itemize}
838	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
839	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
840	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
841	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
842	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
843	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
844	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
845	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
846	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
847	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
848	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
849	    Route~A.
850	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
851	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
852	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
853	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
854	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
855	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
856	\end{itemize}
857	
858	\section{Internal-consistency check}
859	\label{sec:pic0_consistency_check}
860	
861	The ten declaration blocks form a connected \(\uses\) chain rooted in
862	the sibling chapters \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability},
863	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, and \cref{chap:Genus}:
864	
865	\begin{itemize}
866	  \item \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} is a structural
867	    Archon-internal abstract substrate; no cross-chapter
868	    \texttt{\textbackslash uses} pointers.
869	  \item \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup},
870	    \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism},
871	    \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible},
872	    \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} all use
873	    \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}.
874	    \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} additionally
875	    uses \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} (the clopen
876	    inclusion whose homomorphism status it asserts).
877	  \item \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} uses
878	    \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} and
879	    \cref{def:pic_scheme} (from
880	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
881	  \item \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} uses \cref{def:pic_scheme}
882	    (from \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) and
883	    \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} (from \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}).
884	  \item \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety} uses
885	    \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme},
886	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup},
887	    \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism},
888	    \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible},
889	    \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}, and
890	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} (from
891	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
892	  \item \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus} uses
893	    \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety} and
894	    \cref{def:genus} (from \cref{chap:Genus}).
895	  \item \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} uses
896	    \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
897	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup},
898	    \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}, and
899	    \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.
900	\end{itemize}
901	
902	The cross-chapter labels \texttt{def:pic\_scheme},
903	\texttt{thm:fga\_pic\_representability},
904	\texttt{thm:pic\_is\_group\_scheme},
905	\texttt{def:hilbert\_polynomial}, and \texttt{def:genus} all exist in
906	sibling chapters (verified against
907	\texttt{Picard\_FGAPicRepresentability.tex},
908	\texttt{Picard\_QuotScheme.tex}, and \texttt{Genus.tex} at writing
909	time).
910

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
126	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176	
177	\section{Lean encoding}
178	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
179	
180	The Lean target file is the new
181	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
182	this chapter targets is the theorem
183	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
184	notation):
185	\[
186	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
187	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
188	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
189	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
190	\]
191	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
192	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
193	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
195	in the three-step pattern above (codim \(\geq 2\) from A.4.a + pure-codim \(1\) from A.4.a
196	\(\Rightarrow\) empty indeterminacy locus).
197	
198	The Mathlib namespace target is
199	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
200	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
201	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
202	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
203	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
204	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
205	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
206	
207	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
208	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
209	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
210	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
211	
212	\section{Out of scope}
213	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
214	
215	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
216	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
217	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
218	\begin{itemize}
219	  \item \textbf{The codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
220	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
221	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
222	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
223	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
224	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
225	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
226	    (Milne~\(\equiv\) Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
227	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
228	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
229	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
230	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
231	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
232	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
233	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
234	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) is constant'',
235	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
236	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
237	    current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case
238	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
239	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
240	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
241	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
242	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
243	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
244	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
245	    \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to \(C^{(g)} \to A\).
246	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
247	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
248	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
249	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
250	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
251	\end{itemize}
