43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	
95	\leanok
96	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
97	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
99	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
100	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
101	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
103	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
104	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
105	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
106	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
107	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
108	  % \varphi$."
109	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
110	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
111	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
112	  \[
113	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
114	  \]
115	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
116	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
117	  \[
118	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
119	  \]
120	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
121	
122	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
123	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
124	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
125	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
126	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
127	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
128	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
129	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
130	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
131	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
132	\end{definition}
133	
134	\begin{definition}
135	
136	
137	\leanok
138	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
139	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
141	  
142	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
143	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
144	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
145	  % PDF page 22).
146	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
147	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
148	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
149	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
150	  conclusion).}
151	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
152	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
153	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
154	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
155	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
156	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
157	
158	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
159	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
160	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
161	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
162	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
163	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
164	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
165	  is empty) as a single line.
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
169	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
170	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
171	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
172	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
173	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
174	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
175	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
176	\end{definition}
177	
178	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
179	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
180	
181	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
182	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
183	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
184	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
185	
186	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
187	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
188	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
189	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
190	
191	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
192	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
193	
194	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
195	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
196	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
197	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
198	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
199	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
200	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
201	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
202	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
203	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
204	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
205	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
206	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
207	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
208	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
209	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
210	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
211	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
212	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	
217	\leanok
218	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
219	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
221	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
222	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
223	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
224	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
225	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
226	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
227	  \(S_M\)-module.
228	\end{lemma}
229	
230	\begin{proof}
231	  The canonical comparison map
232	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
233	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
234	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
235	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
236	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
237	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
238	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
239	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
240	  the conclusion.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{lemma}
244	
245	
246	\leanok
247	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
248	  the relative dimension]
249	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
251	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
252	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
253	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
254	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
255	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
256	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
257	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
258	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
259	\end{lemma}
260	
261	\begin{proof}
262	  Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
264	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
265	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
266	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
267	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
268	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
269	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
270	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
271	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
272	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
273	  ranks equal \(n\), so
274	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
275	\end{proof}
276	
277	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
278	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
279	
280	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
281	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
282	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
283	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
284	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
285	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
286	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
287	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
288	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
289	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
290	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
291	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
292	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
293	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
294	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
295	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
296	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
297	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
298	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
299	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
300	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
301	named iter-199+ prover targets.
302	
303	\begin{lemma}
304	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
305	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
306	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
307	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
308	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
309	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
310	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
311	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
312	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
313	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
314	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
315	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
316	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
317	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
318	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
319	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
320	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
321	  \[
322	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
323	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
324	  \]
325	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
326	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
327	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
328	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
329	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
330	
331	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
332	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
333	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
334	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
335	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
336	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
337	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
338	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
339	\end{lemma}
340	
341	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-prime-polynomial-ring}
342	% we know that $r = \text{trdeg}_k\ \kappa(\mathfrak p)$ is equal to the
343	% dimension of $V(\mathfrak p)$. Pick any maximal chain of primes
344	% $\mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathfrak p_r$
345	% starting with $\mathfrak p$ in $S$. This has length $r$ by Lemma
346	% \ref{lemma-dimension-spell-it-out}. Let $\mathfrak q_j$, $j \in J$ be
347	% the minimal primes of $S$ which are contained in $\mathfrak p$. These
348	% correspond $1-1$ to minimal primes in $S_{\mathfrak p}$ via the rule
349	% $\mathfrak q_j \mapsto \mathfrak q_jS_{\mathfrak p}$. By Lemma
350	% \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-over-field} we know that
351	% $\dim_x(X)$ is equal to the maximum of the dimensions of the rings
352	% $S/\mathfrak q_j$. For each $j$ pick a maximal chain of primes
353	% $\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
354	% \mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p$. Then $\dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) =
355	% \max_{j \in J} s(j)$. Now, each chain
356	% $\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
357	% \mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots
358	% \subset \mathfrak p_r$ is a maximal chain in $S/\mathfrak q_j$, and by
359	% what was said before we have $\dim_x(X) = \max_{j \in J} r + s(j)$.
360	% The lemma follows."
361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
363	  This is exactly Stacks tag~\texttt{00OE}
364	  (\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}); the proof is a
365	  citation of the dimension theory of finitely generated algebras over a
366	  field. By Noether normalisation the finite-type \(k\)-algebra \(S\) is
367	  finite over a polynomial subalgebra, so the going-up / catenary
368	  dimension theory of such algebras applies. Write
369	  \(r = \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); this transcendence
370	  degree is exactly the dimension of the closed subvariety
371	  \(\overline{\{x\}} = V(\mathfrak q)\). A maximal chain of primes
372	  through \(\mathfrak q\) therefore splits into a
373	  length-\(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) part below \(\mathfrak q\) (recording
374	  the local codimension, i.e.\ the height of \(\mathfrak q\) in the
375	  relevant irreducible component) and a length-\(r\) part above it
376	  (recording the dimension of \(\overline{\{x\}}\)). Taking the maximum
377	  over the minimal primes contained in \(\mathfrak q\) yields
378	  \[
379	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + r
380	    = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q),
381	  \]
382	  which is the asserted formula.
383	
384	  For the geometric specialisation matching the Lean target, take
385	  \(k = \bar k\) algebraically closed and \(S\) standard smooth of
386	  relative dimension \(n\), so that \(\dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S) = n\) via
387	  \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (the free
388	  K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}\) has rank \(n\), which
389	  pins the relative dimension). The formula then gives
390	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak
391	  q)\). At a closed point \(\mathfrak q\) is maximal, so by the
392	  Nullstellensatz \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\) and
393	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\); hence
394	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S) = n\).
395	\end{proof}

397	\begin{lemma}
398	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
399	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
401	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
402	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
403	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
404	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
405	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
406	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
407	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
408	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
409	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
410	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
411	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
412	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
414	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
415	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
416	  % $S'$-modules
417	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
418	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
419	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
420	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
422	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
423	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
424	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
425	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
426	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
427	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
428	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
429	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
430	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
431	  \[
432	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
433	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
434	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
435	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
436	  \]
437	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
438	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
439	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
440	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
441	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
442	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
443	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
444	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
445	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
446	  \[
447	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
448	  \]
449	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
450	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
451	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
452	\end{lemma}

454	\begin{lemma}
455	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
456	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
457	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
458	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
459	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
460	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
461	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
462	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
463	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
464	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
465	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
466	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
467	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
468	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
469	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
470	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
471	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
472	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
473	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
474	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
475	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
476	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
477	  dimension equals \(1\).
478	\end{lemma}
479	
480	\begin{proof}
481	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
482	  \begin{enumerate}
483	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
484	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
485	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
486	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
487	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
488	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
489	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
490	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
491	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
492	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
493	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
494	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
495	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
496	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
497	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
498	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
499	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
500	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
501	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
502	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
503	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
504	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
505	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
506	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
507	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
508	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
509	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
510	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
511	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
512	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
513	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
514	      the conclusion.
515	  \end{enumerate}
516	\end{proof}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}

402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}

480	\section{Lean encoding}
481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}
523	
524	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
525	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
526	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
527	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
528	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).

1124	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
1125	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
1126	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
1127	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
1128	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
1129	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
1130	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
1131	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
1132	
1133	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
1134	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
1135	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
1136	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
1137	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
1138	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
1139	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
1140	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
1141	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
1142	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
1143	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
1144	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
1145	\end{proof}

1147	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
1148	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
1149	
1150	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
1151	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
1152	point.
1153	
1154	\begin{theorem}
1155	
1156	
1157	\leanok
1158	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
1159	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
1160	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
1161	  
1162	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
1163	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
1164	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
1165	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
1166	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
1167	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
1168	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
1169	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
1170	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
1171	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
1172	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1173	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
1174	
1175	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1176	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
1177	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
1178	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
1179	  in \(X\).
1180	\end{theorem}
1181	
1182	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
1183	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
1184	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
1185	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
1186	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
1187	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
1188	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
1189	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
1190	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
1191	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
1192	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
1193	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
1194	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
1195	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
1196	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
1197	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
1198	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
1199	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
1200	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
1201	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
1202	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
1203	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
1204	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
1205	% which is a contradiction."
1206	\begin{proof}
1207	  
1208	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
1209	
1210	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
1211	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
1212	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
1213	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
1214	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
1215	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
1216	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
1217	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
1218	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
1219	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
1220	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
1221	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
1222	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
1223	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
1224	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
1225	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
1226	
1227	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
1228	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
1229	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
1230	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
1231	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
1232	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
1233	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
1234	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
1235	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
1236	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
1237	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
1238	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
1239	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
1240	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
1241	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
1242	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
1243	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
1244	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
1245	
1246	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
1247	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
1248	  agree on all of \(X\).
1249	\end{proof}
1250	
1251	\begin{remark}
1252	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
1253	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
1254	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
1255	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
1256	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
1257	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
1258	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
1259	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
1260	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
1261	
1262	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
1263	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
1264	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
1265	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
1266	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
1267	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
1268	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
1269	\end{remark}
