1335	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
1336	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
1337	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
1338	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
1339	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
1340	\begin{proof}
1341	  
1342	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
1343	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
1344	
1345	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
1346	  \[
1347	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
1348	    \qquad
1349	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
1350	  \]
1351	  via the composition
1352	  \[
1353	    U \times U
1354	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
1355	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
1356	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
1357	  \]
1358	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
1359	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
1360	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
1361	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
1362	
1363	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
1364	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
1365	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
1366	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
1367	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
1368	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
1369	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
1370	  identity
1371	  \[
1372	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
1373	    \qquad u \in W',
1374	  \]
1375	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
1376	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
1377	
1378	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
1379	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
1380	  \(K(X \times X)\):
1381	  \[
1382	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
1383	  \]
1384	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
1385	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
1386	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
1387	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
1388	
1389	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
1390	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
1391	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
1392	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
1393	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
1394	  \[
1395	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
1396	  \]
1397	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
1398	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
1399	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
1400	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
1401	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
1402	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
1403	  \[
1404	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
1405	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
1406	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1407	  \]
1408	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1409	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1410	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1411	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1412	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1413	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1414	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1415	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1416	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1417	
1418	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1419	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
1420	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
1421	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1422	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
1423	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1424	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1425	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1426	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1427	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1428	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1429	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1430	\end{proof}
1431	
1432	\begin{remark}
1433	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1434	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1435	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1436	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1437	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1438	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1439	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1440	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1441	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1442	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1443	\end{remark}
1444	
1445	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1446	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1447	
1448	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1449	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1450	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1451	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1452	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1453	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1454	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1455	
1456	\begin{theorem}
1457	
1458	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
1459	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1460	  
1461	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
1462	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
1463	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
1464	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
1465	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
1466	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
1467	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
1468	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
1469	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
1470	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
1471	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
1472	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
1473	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
1474	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
1475	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
1476	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
1477	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
1478	  % NOTE (iter-194 writer): no corresponding Lean declaration found in
1479	  % CodimOneExtension.lean --- iter-195 must either rename to match an
1480	  % existing decl or add a new Lean stub. (The iter-179 detach was
1481	  % structural, not a \lean{...} typo: the substantive Hartshorne-II.6
1482	  % ord_W >= 0 reformulation requires the
1483	  % Scheme.RationalMap-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
1484	  % not in Mathlib at commit b80f227 and is not yet built in this
1485	  % project. The weaker iter-179 reshuffle Lean declaration
1486	  % mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point is blueprint-pinned
1487	  % at \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} as the honest
1488	  % fallback; thm:weil_divisor_obstruction here remains unpinned at the
1489	  % \lean{...} level until the genuine pullback decl lands.)
1490	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
1491	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
1492	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
1493	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
1494	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
1495	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
1496	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
1497	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
1498	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
1499	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
1500	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
1501	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
1502	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
1503	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
1504	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
1505	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
1506	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
1507	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
1508	  \begin{enumerate}
1509	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
1510	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
1511	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
1512	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
1513	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
1514	      satisfying
1515	      \[
1516	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
1517	      \]
1518	  \end{enumerate}
1519	
1520	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1521	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
1522	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
1523	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
1524	\end{theorem}
1525	
1526	\begin{proof}
1527	  
1528	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
1529	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
1530	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
1531	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
1532	  \[
1533	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
1534	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1535	  \]
1536	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
1537	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
1538	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
1539	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
1540	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
1541	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
1542	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
1543	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
1544	
1545	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
1546	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
1547	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
1548	\end{proof}
1549	
1550	\begin{remark}
1551	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
1552	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
1553	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
1554	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
1555	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
1556	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1557	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
1558	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
1559	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
1560	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
1561	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
1562	\end{remark}
1563	
1564	\begin{lemma}
1565	
1566	
1567	\leanok
1568	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
1569	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
1570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
1571	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
1572	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
1573	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
1574	  following are equivalent:
1575	  \begin{enumerate}
1576	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
1577	      \(W\)).
1578	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
1579	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
1580	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
1581	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
1582	  \end{enumerate}
1583	\end{lemma}
1584	
1585	\begin{proof}
1586	
1587	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
1588	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
1589	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
1590	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
1591	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
1592	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
1593	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
1594	  the unfolded definition.
1595	\end{proof}
1596	
1597	\begin{remark}
1598	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
1599	  \textbf{Relationship to \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
1600	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
1601	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
1602	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
1603	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
1604	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
1605	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
1606	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
1607	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
1608	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
1609	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
1610	  history.

1725	\section{Out of scope}
1726	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1727	
1728	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1729	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1730	scope:
1731	\begin{itemize}
1732	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1733	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1734	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1735	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1736	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1737	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1738	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1739	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1740	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1741	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1742	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1743	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1744	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1745	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1746	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1747	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1748	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1749	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1750	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1751	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1752	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1753	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1754	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1755	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1756	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1757	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1758	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1759	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1760	    introduced.
1761	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1762	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1763	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1764	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1765	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1766	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1767	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
1768	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1769	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1770	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1771	\end{itemize}

3383	\begin{proof}
3384	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map}
3385	  Write \(\Phi\) for the natural transformation whose naturality square (in the two module
3386	  arguments \(M, N\), along morphisms \(a : M \to M'\) and \(b : N \to N'\)) is to be shown
3387	  to commute; \(\Phi\) is assembled from the reconciliation isomorphism
3388	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) (the intermediate construction inside
3389	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}), so its components are morphisms of presheaves of modules.
3390	  The square is closed at the categorical (tensor-of-morphisms) level, without any descent to
3391	  sections or to elements.
3392	
3393	  \emph{Step 1: a single tensor-of-morphisms presentation.} The \(\mathrm{hom}\) leg of
3394	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) is rewritten as a \emph{single} tensor of morphisms of the
3395	  form \(a.\mathrm{map}(\eta_P \otimes \eta_Q)\), where
3396	  \(\eta = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}).\mathrm{unit}\) is the unit
3397	  \(\mathbf{1} \Rightarrow (\text{sheafify}) \circ (\text{inclusion})\) of the sheafification
3398	  adjunction and \(a\) is the sheafification functor. The essential point is that all factors
3399	  are kept inside \emph{one} monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) --- the
3400	  canonical presentation as a composite with the forgetful functor \(\mathtt{forget}_2\) ---
3401	  so that the bifunctoriality law of \(\otimes\) can be applied consistently within one
3402	  monoidal structure.
3403	
3404	  \emph{Step 2: merge the two functor-image factors.} Both composites around the square are
3405	  images, under the sheafification functor, of a tensor of morphisms. The two functor-image
3406	  factors are merged into a single image of a composite by functoriality,
3407	  \(F(g) \circ F(h) = F(g \circ h)\).
3408	
3409	  \emph{Step 3: bifunctoriality and unit naturality.} The resulting equality of two tensors of
3410	  morphisms is closed by the bifunctoriality (interchange) law
3411	  \[
3412	    (f_1 \otimes f_2) \circ (g_1 \otimes g_2) \;=\; (f_1 \circ g_1) \otimes (f_2 \circ g_2),
3413	  \]
3414	  applied within the single chosen monoidal structure, together with the two
3415	  single-component naturality squares of the sheafification unit \(\eta\), one in each module
3416	  argument:

3419	    \qquad

3423	  single-component squares through the interchange law equates the two tensors of morphisms,

1031	
1032	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1033	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1034	
1035	\begin{definition}
1036	
1037	\leanok
1038	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1039	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1040	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1041	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1042	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1043	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1044	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1045	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1046	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1047	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1048	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1049	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1050	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1051	\end{definition}
1052	
1053	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
1054	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
1055	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
1056	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1057	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
1058	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
1059	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
1060	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
1061	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1062	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1063	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1064	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
1065	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
1066	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
1067	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
1068	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
1069	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
1070	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
1071	\begin{corollary}
1072	
1073	\leanok
1074	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1075	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1076	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1077	  
1078	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1079	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1080	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1081	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1082	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1083	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1084	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1085	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1086	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1087	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1088	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1089	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1090	\end{corollary}
1091	
1092	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1093	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1094	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1095	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1096	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1097	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	  
1100	  
1101	  
1102	  
1103	  
1104	\leanok
1105	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1106	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1107	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1108	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1109	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1110	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1111	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1112	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1113	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1114	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1115	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1116	
1117	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1118	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1119	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1120	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1121	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1122	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1123	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1124	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1125	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1126	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1127	\end{proof}
1128	
1129	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1130	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1131	
1132	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1133	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1134	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1135	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1136	
1137	\begin{enumerate}
1138	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1139	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1140	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1141	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1142	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1143	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1144	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1145	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1146	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1147	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1148	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1149	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch

12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are

30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{\(\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1\)}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}
324	
325	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
326	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
327	
328	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
329	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
330	The private helper
331	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
332	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
333	\texttt{sorry} of the form
334	\[
335	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
336	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
337	\]
338	After this chapter's
339	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
340	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1\)
344	    is discharged by
345	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\) (the standing codim-\(1\) hypothesis
346	    on \(z\)) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1\) (also from the bridge, since
349	    \(1 \leq 1\)) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct \(\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A \(\to\) IsRegularLocalRing \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\)} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because \(Y\) is a prime divisor of \(X\) (so
371	\(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1\) by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-\(1\) irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\), so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-\(1\) infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-\(1\) point has
379	Krull dim \(\leq 1\)'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-\(1\)
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.
385	
386	\section{Lean encoding}
387	\label{sec:coheight_lean_encoding}
388	
389	The Lean target file is the new
390	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}. The declarations to
391	introduce, in order:
392	
393	\begin{enumerate}
394	  \item \texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding} ---
395	    \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}. Signature (informal): for
396	    \(X\) a topological space, \(U \subseteq X\) open, and \(z \in U\),
397	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = \operatorname{coheight}_{U}(\langle z, hz\rangle)\).
398	    Tools: \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} for the
399	    chain-lifting direction; \texttt{Subtype.val} continuity for the
400	    embedding direction. Estimated \(\sim 20\)--\(30\)~LOC.
401	  \item \texttt{Order.coheight\_spec\_eq\_height\_primeSpectrum} ---
402	    \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}. Signature (informal):
403	    for \(R\) a \texttt{CommRingCat} and \(p : \Spec R\) a point, coheight of \(p\)
404	    in \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\). Tools:
405	    \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447),
406	    \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso}, \texttt{Order.height\_toDual}
407	    (\texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Estimated \(\sim 10\)--\(15\)~LOC.
408	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim\_stalk\_eq\_coheight} ---
409	    \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. Signature (informal): for
410	    \(X : \texttt{Scheme}\) and \(z : X\),
411	    $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,z) =
412	    \operatorname{coheight}\,z$. Five-step assembly per the proof above.
413	    Tools: \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset},
414	    \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk},
415	    \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height},
416	    \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}, plus the two lemmas above.
417	    Estimated \(\sim 30\)--\(50\)~LOC.
418	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE\_of\_coheight\_eq\_one} ---
419	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}. Signature (informal):
420	    given \(X : \texttt{Scheme}\), \(z : X\), and \(\operatorname{coheight}\,z = 1\),
421	    derive \texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk z)}. Trivial corollary
422	    of (3). Estimated \(\sim 10\)~LOC. Lemma vs.\ instance phrasing: the
423	    \texttt{coheight\,z = 1} hypothesis is not a typeclass, so this lands as
424	    a plain lemma. Downstream files that have the codim-\(1\) hypothesis in
425	    hand invoke it explicitly; downstream typeclass infrastructure that
426	    bundles ``\(z\) is a codim-\(1\) point'' into a typeclass (if any is added
427	    later) can re-export as an instance.
428	\end{enumerate}
429

432	\begin{itemize}
433	  \item \texttt{Order.coheight}, \texttt{Order.height}, \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso},

453	\section{Out of scope}
454	\label{sec:coheight_out_of_scope}
455	
456	This chapter packages \emph{only} the coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge and its
457	codim-\(1\) specialisation. The following adjacent content is explicitly out
458	of scope:
459	\begin{itemize}
460	  \item \textbf{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) regular stalks).}
461	    This is a separate sub-project of \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC, tracked in
462	    \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 1. The directive
463	    flags it as iter-200+ work. The natural project landing site, when
464	    attacked, is a standalone file
465	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/SmoothToRegular.lean} (not part of
466	    this chapter).
467	  \item \textbf{The codim-\(1\) valuative criterion / Milne Lemma 3.3
468	    difference-map construction.} This is the second multi-iter sub-project
469	    flagged in \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 3
470	    (\(\sim 300\)--\(500\)~LOC of project-side new infrastructure); see
471	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} in
472	    \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} for the chapter that owns it.
473	  \item \textbf{Refactoring \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} to drop its
474	    explicit \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 \_]} instance argument.} This is a
475	    downstream consequence of \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
476	    that lives in \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}; the present chapter
477	    only \emph{enables} the refactor, it does not perform it.
478	  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PRs.} The two lemmas
479	    \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} and
480	    \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} are natural candidates for
481	    upstreaming (their natural Mathlib homes are
482	    \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension} and a new
483	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Coheight} respectively); the chapter
484	    flags this as an observation but does not pursue it.
485	  \item \textbf{General codim-\(n\) specialisations.} Only the codim-\(1\)
486	    instance is packaged here, because that is the consumer-facing case.
487	    A general \texttt{ringKrullDimLE\_of\_coheight\_le} or
488	    \texttt{ringKrullDimLE\_of\_coheight\_eq} family of lemmas can be added
489	    iteratively if downstream demand emerges, but is not required for any
490	    A.4.* sub-row at the time of writing.
491	\end{itemize}
